channel.title: hugo on Blog
channel.description: Recent content in hugo on Blog

item.title: The strongest principle of the blog's growth lies in the human choice to deploy it
item.description: The strongest principle of growth lies in the human choice1 Build Hugo With GitHub Action It is time to put our blog on the internet. Source code is stored on GitHub so hosting it on the GitHub Pages seems like easiest way to achive that. Good starting point will be just using official Hugo docs. My plan is to use my own domain name instead of provided one and also we will need to configure our workflow to properly build TailwindCSS which is used in this project.

item.title: If you're going to blog about something, do it with style
item.description: If you’re going to do something, do it with style!1 Where are my styles? We’ve just installed TailwindCSS to our project and ready to start adding styles to make it look better, but for some reason it now looks worse than before we installed it. Before After The Preflight configuration is a culprit who “stole” our styles. I’ve dealt with it on one of my different series Vite vue ts tailwind template.

item.title: To fly you need a tailwind
item.description: Why TailwindCSS In one of my previous projects, I fell in love with TailwindCSS. I know that this is a very controversial framework for some, but for me, personally, as a backend developer of 11 years, it was the first time I had a fun time adding styles to the frontend. I even created vue-ts template with it, as a part of another articles’ series. Asset management in Hugo There are few ways to add CSS library to our blog, because Hugo supports multiple ways to do asset management.

item.title: Basic content render
item.description: Sane defaults Previously in this series, we created a new Hugo site and copied scaffold of the default theme to our layout folder. Now let’s update our templates to have great HTML defaults. I would suggest reading the great article Basic HTML5 Template: Use This HTML Boilerplate as a Starter for Any Web Dev Project which dives a little deeper into this topic. But for our case, we will just base our default template on Vite’s boilerplate template for Vue.

item.title: Hugo no theme
item.description: Using Hugo without theme In quick start guide and many online tutorials, new projects are advised to use one of the existing themes or create a new one. But after reading official docs, I’ve decided not to use any theme in my previous blog version. I am going to do the same for this new project. If in the future some of my functionality will look like it could be decoupled from the blog, I will create a theme but for now, I do not see any need for one.

item.title: hugo new site
item.description: git init I started by dogfooding1 my template repository. It allows me to use terraform to manage GitHub repository declaratively. If you’re not familiar with “Infrastructure as Code” I highly recommend giving it a try, the declarative approach to configuration management and resource provisioning makes repetitive tasks so much easier. Also, it already has editorconfig and pre-commit configuration. After I initialized my blog with terraform and configured git to use created repo as a remote.

item.title: Building the plane while flying it
item.description: Build the plane while you’re flying it1 New beginnings In the February 2022 I finally decided to create my personal blog. I’ve chosen the Hugo as a framework for building it. On the landing page, it bravely declares: The world’s fastest framework for building websites. With its amazing speed and flexibility, Hugo makes building websites fun again. and after using it, I can confidently say it really lives up to what it promises.
